title: The Manufacturing Clock of a Self-Replicating Fleet Is a Rounding Error on Galactic Settlement
abstract: A programme of self-replicating space manufacturing is usually modelled one scale at a time: a single factory that copies itself, a local fleet that disperses across a solar system, or a settlement front that crosses a galaxy. We thread one sourced factory through all three folds, so that a quantity every scale needs, the time to build one copy, is derived once rather than assumed three times over. The result is a clean cross-scale statement. The copy time that sets a local fleet's doubling clock, about 582 days for a lunar-regolith seed, is at galactic scale a negligible manufacturing tax: switching the derived per-star dwell on lengthens the roughly two-million-year time a powered front takes to fill a stellar field by under one part in fifty thousand. Because that copy time is a lunar-factory stand-in for an interstellar probe whose own bill of materials is not yet known, we do not defend its exact value; we show the conclusion survives not knowing it. The tax grows in proportion to the copy time, and for the powered galactic fill it stays below one percent until the copy time is a few hundred times its nominal value, so an error of two orders of magnitude in the build cadence does not change the verdict. That margin is smaller for faster fronts, which is the honest boundary of the claim: measured directly, seed by seed, the dwell already costs a median of about a third of a percent of exploration time for the fastest slingshot policy, and is within seed-to-seed noise for the slower one, so there the copy time need grow only a few-fold to matter. The picture is therefore robust but bounded: manufacturing time is the binding clock for a local fleet, where transit is days, and a small, quantified tax on interstellar exploration, where transit is millennia, vanishing for a powered cruise and climbing toward a percent as the front is driven faster.
